name: balanced-manual-comparison-v2
description: A manual side-by-side comparison corpus of 24 fully synthetic writing tasks across four workflows: creation from scratch, rewrite, review, and outline.
sizeTierBasis: Requested total output length, including headings, labels, and subject lines.

cases.title: Dawnlist Freelancer Ad
cases.prompt:
Creative brief:
- Create a paid social ad for Dawnlist, a fictional planning app for freelancers who feel scattered at the start of the workday.
- Product facts: users choose three priorities the evening before, check them off the next day, and receive no notifications; the price is $4 per month.
- Goal: earn a click to learn more without shaming the audience.
- Voice: crisp, understanding, quietly confident.

Write 35-55 words. Open with a specific, relatable hook, connect the product mechanic to the reader's morning, and end with a clear call to action. Use at most one emoji. Do not invent results, guarantees, discounts, reviews, or urgency. Return only the finished ad.
cases.outputConstraint: 35-55 words; relatable hook, product mechanic, and clear CTA; at most one emoji; no invented claims, proof, discounts, or urgency; output only.

cases.title: Closed Makers Room Sign
cases.prompt:
Rewrite the draft sign below so it is warm, direct, and easy to scan. Preserve every fact. Output only the finished sign in 45-65 words.

Draft:
NOTICE. The Makers Room cannot be used today because the ventilation inspection is occurring. People with reservations will get an email. The Print Studio on level 2 is still open for drop-in use until 5 p.m. Ask at the welcome desk if you need assistance.
cases.outputConstraint: Output only the finished sign in 45-65 words; preserve all supplied facts.

cases.title: The Missing Floor
cases.prompt:
Premise:
An office elevator stops at a thirteenth floor that is absent from every directory. The doors open onto an unlit corridor. A wet umbrella is waiting upright outside, though the building has been sealed all weekend. The first-person narrator is alone and must decide whether to take it.

Create a complete 55-75-word microfiction story from this premise. Build unease through concrete sensory detail, include exactly one short line of dialogue, and end on the narrator's decision rather than an explanation. Do not add gore, a named villain, or a dream reveal. Give it no title. Return only the story.
cases.outputConstraint: 55-75 words; first person; concrete sensory detail; exactly one short line of dialogue; end on a decision; no title, gore, named villain, dream reveal, or commentary.

cases.title: PebbleList Store Listing Review
cases.prompt:
Review the fictional app-store listing below for clarity, credibility, differentiation, and likely reader questions. Do not rewrite it. Output only a concise editorial review in 130-190 words total, including the requested headings, organized as Strengths, Friction, and Highest-impact changes. Identify specific language from the draft by paraphrase or short quotation; do not make claims about actual store policy or market performance.

Listing draft:
PebbleList makes every day effortless. The world's most delightful list app transforms chaos into calm with beautiful colors and powerful simplicity. Create unlimited lists, sort items into three priorities, and share a list with up to four other PebbleList users. The free plan includes five active lists. PebbleList Plus costs $2.99 per month and adds unlimited active lists, recurring items, and image attachments. Your lists sync when you are online; offline edits sync the next time you connect. No ads. Start organizing your best life today.
cases.outputConstraint: Output only a 130-190-word editorial review total, including the three requested headings; do not rewrite the listing.

cases.title: Heron & Field Cracker Box
cases.prompt:
Product facts:
- Heron & Field is a fictional bakery brand.
- The product is a thin rosemary oat cracker baked in small batches.
- It contains rolled oats, wheat flour, olive oil, rosemary, and sea salt.
- The box contains 18 crackers and costs $6.
- Audience: adults assembling an unfussy snack board.

Create 45-70 words total of back-of-box copy, including the headline. Include a short headline, appetizing but credible body copy, and one simple serving suggestion. Voice: warm, modern, unpretentious. Do not make health, sourcing, environmental, or comparative claims and do not invent ingredients or certifications. Return only the finished packaging copy.
cases.outputConstraint: 45-70 words total including the headline; short headline, credible body, and one serving suggestion; warm and unpretentious; no invented ingredients, certifications, or health, sourcing, environmental, or comparative claims; output only.

cases.title: Kite Lamp Warranty Card
cases.prompt:
Rewrite the warranty-card draft below in plain, reassuring language. Make the next step unmistakable without inventing coverage or legal terms. Output only the finished card in 110-160 words.

Draft:
Thank you for purchasing the fictional Kite reading lamp from Aster & Vale. You should register it at asterandvale.example/register, but registration is not required for warranty service. The lamp has a two-year limited warranty covering defects in materials and workmanship. It does not cover accidental damage, misuse, unauthorized repairs, or normal wear. If something is wrong, email care@asterandvale.example with the order number, a description, and a photo or short video. The support team replies within two business days. Customers should not mail the lamp unless support provides a prepaid label and return instructions.
cases.outputConstraint: Output only the finished warranty card in 110-160 words; use only the stated coverage and process.

cases.title: Patchbay First-Run Welcome
cases.prompt:
Creative brief:
Patchbay is a fictional browser tool that lets small creative teams collect webpage screenshots, annotate them, and group them into shared boards. A new user named Casey has just created a free workspace but has not added a teammate or saved a screenshot. The desired first action is to install the browser extension and capture one page. There is no trial deadline and no sales call. Support is available at help@patchbay.example.

Write a welcome email of 100-135 words total, including the subject line. Make the first action feel concrete and low-pressure, explain why completing it is useful, and include one primary call to action. Voice: friendly, capable, concise. Do not invent features, customer counts, time estimates, incentives, or urgency. Return only the finished email.
cases.outputConstraint: 100-135 words total including the subject line; one concrete first action, its usefulness, and one primary CTA; friendly and concise; no invented features, proof, estimates, incentives, or urgency; output only.

cases.title: Lantern Row Night-Market Audio Walk
cases.prompt:
Create a production-ready outline for a fictional 12-minute location-based audio walk called "After Closing: Lantern Row." There is no prose draft to revise. Use only the brief below. Output only the outline in 330-480 words total, including all headings, labels, and table text. Include a one-sentence listener promise, the dramatic progression, six numbered stops with approximate duration and purpose, sound-design cues, transitions, accessibility provisions, and a closing beat. Do not write the finished narration or invent historical facts.

Brief:
Lantern Row is a fictional covered night market that operates from 5 p.m. to midnight. The audio walk is heard after the stalls close, during a monthly ticketed tour. Its subject is the invisible labor that resets the market between midnight and dawn. The six available locations are the locked east gate, the empty spice aisle, the wash station, the crate exchange, the roof garden stairwell, and the west loading bay. Available voices are a cleaning supervisor, a produce vendor, a crate coordinator, a maintenance apprentice, and the tour host. Supplied facts: ninety-six stalls operate on a full market night; vendors separate reusable crates from cardboard; wash-water is filtered before entering the municipal system; herbs from the small roof garden are used by three food stalls; deliveries begin at 3:40 a.m.; the reset crew finishes at 4:15 a.m. The tone should be intimate and observant, not promotional. The listener must never enter working areas or block staff. Provide a text transcript and indicate non-speech sounds in brackets. The route is step-free except for the optional roof-garden stairwell; include an equivalent ground-level segment. No listener should be asked to close their eyes while walking.
cases.outputConstraint: Output only a 330-480-word production outline total, including all headings, labels, and table text; no finished narration or invented facts.

cases.title: Night Shelf Donation Appeal
cases.prompt:
Campaign brief:
Night Shelf is a fictional volunteer-run program that places free paperback books in three overnight hospital waiting rooms. A $12 donation purchases four new paperbacks selected from staff requests. The current campaign goal is $3,000 by October 31 to refresh all three shelves. Donations are optional, are not described as tax-deductible, and do not fund medical care. Audience: local residents who value small, practical acts of comfort.

Create 140-185 words total for a donation landing page, including the headline and any call-to-action label. Include a headline, a brief emotional opening grounded in the setting, a transparent explanation of what the money supports, and a direct donation call to action. Keep the tone humane and specific without guilt, pressure, patient stories, or claims about medical outcomes. Do not invent partners, statistics, matching funds, or testimonials. Return only the finished page copy.
cases.outputConstraint: 140-185 words total including the headline and any CTA label; grounded opening, transparent use of funds, and direct donation CTA; humane without guilt or pressure; no invented stories, outcomes, partners, statistics, matching funds, or testimonials; output only.

cases.title: Juniper Clinic Holiday Voicemail
cases.prompt:
Rewrite the fictional clinic voicemail below so callers hear the most useful information in a calm, logical order. Preserve every operational detail and do not add medical advice. Output only the spoken script in 130-190 words.

Draft:
You have reached Juniper Community Clinic. We are closed on Monday, September 7, for staff training and will reopen Tuesday at 8 a.m. If you have a life-threatening emergency, call your local emergency number. Prescription refill requests can be left through the patient portal, but requests received during the closure will be reviewed Tuesday and may take two business days. Appointments can be scheduled or canceled at juniperclinic.example. Patients with Tuesday laboratory appointments should arrive at the time already listed in their confirmation. Our dental desk has a separate line at 555-0142 and will also reopen Tuesday. Please leave a message after the tone for non-urgent billing questions, including your name, callback number, and invoice number, but do not include private medical details.
cases.outputConstraint: Output only the voicemail script in 130-190 words; retain all operational and privacy details without adding medical advice.

cases.title: Small Signals Show Trailer
cases.prompt:
Show concept:
Small Signals is a fictional weekly podcast about overlooked design choices that shape everyday behavior. Each 20-minute episode follows one object or system, such as a crosswalk button, a grocery divider, or a library return slot. Host Leena Park interviews the people who make, maintain, or use it. The show launches September 9, with new episodes every Tuesday wherever listeners get podcasts. Audience: curious general listeners, not design specialists.

Create a 120-160-word spoken trailer script for the host. Begin with an intriguing observation or question, establish the show's recurring idea, give listeners a quick sense of the episode range, and end with the launch date and invitation to follow. Make it conversational and vivid when read aloud. Do not invent episode findings, guests, credentials, reviews, sponsors, or sound effects. Do not include a title or stage directions. Return only the script.
cases.outputConstraint: 120-160 spoken words; hook, recurring premise, episode range, launch date, and follow invitation; conversational and vivid; no title, stage directions, or invented findings, guests, credentials, reviews, sponsors, or effects; output only.

cases.title: Northglass Festival Cancellation Statement Review
cases.prompt:
Review the fictional public statement below as a communications editor. Evaluate whether it takes responsibility, answers practical questions, respects affected people, avoids defensiveness, and distinguishes known facts from open questions. Do not rewrite the statement. Output only a 320-450-word review total, including the four section headings: What works, What weakens trust, Missing information, and Revision priorities. Ground every point in the draft or supplied context.

Context:
The fictional Northglass Lantern Festival was canceled forty minutes after gates opened because wind exceeded the safety threshold for suspended installations. Organizers had received a forecast warning that morning but believed the event might proceed. About 2,800 ticket holders had arrived. Shuttle buses continued dropping people at the entrance for twenty minutes after cancellation. Refunds will be automatic within ten business days, including booking fees. Food-vendor purchases are separate and are not refundable by the festival. Organizers will publish a fuller operational review within three weeks. No injuries were reported.

Statement draft:
We are heartbroken to share that circumstances beyond our control forced us to cancel tonight's Northglass Lantern Festival. The safety of our community has always been our number-one priority, and our experienced team did everything possible to deliver the magical evening everyone deserved. Unfortunately, extreme weather appeared unexpectedly, leaving us with no choice. We know some guests experienced delays leaving the grounds, but large events are complicated and our partners worked as quickly as they could. Ticket refunds are being looked into and more details will follow. We ask for patience and kindness toward our hardworking staff, who are just as disappointed as you are. We hope you will remember the joy Northglass has brought the city for eight years instead of judging us by one difficult night. Thank you for your unwavering support.
cases.outputConstraint: Output only a 320-450-word critique total, including the four requested section headings; do not supply replacement copy.

cases.title: Waymark Team Planning Landing Page
cases.prompt:
Create a landing page from scratch for Waymark, a fictional planning tool for teams that do recurring operational work.

Audience: operations leads at companies with 20-200 employees who currently coordinate recurring work through spreadsheets, chat reminders, and calendar events. They are skeptical of adding another tool and care more about reliability than novelty.

Product facts:
- Waymark turns a repeatable process into a shared checklist with owners, due dates, dependencies, and a visible history.
- A process can recur daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly, or on a custom schedule.
- When a step is late or blocked, Waymark alerts the process owner.
- It integrates with Slack and Microsoft Teams.
- It does not perform the work automatically and is not an AI agent.
- Plans cost $8 per active user per month after a 14-day free trial.
- No credit card is required for the trial.
- This company, product, and all facts are fictional.

Write 300-420 words total, including all headings and call-to-action labels. Include, in this order: a headline of at most 10 words; a subhead; a primary call to action; a short problem section; three benefit-led feature sections with descriptive headings; a three-step 'How it works' section; a concise objection-handling section for a reader who thinks their spreadsheet is good enough; and a closing call to action. Make the opening specific and compelling without insulting the audience. Build a coherent argument instead of listing disconnected features. Do not invent customers, testimonials, usage numbers, guarantees, security claims, discounts, or capabilities. Avoid generic phrases such as 'revolutionize your workflow,' 'game changer,' and 'seamless.' Return only the finished landing-page copy.
cases.outputConstraint: 300-420 words total including all headings and CTA labels; required landing-page sections in the specified order; all product and pricing facts preserved; no invented proof, capabilities, guarantees, or security claims; output only the finished copy.

cases.title: Foundry Yard Residency Call
cases.prompt:
Rewrite the fictional open-call draft below into inviting, precise copy for an artist-residency webpage. Lead with the opportunity, make eligibility and expectations easy to understand, and avoid inflated claims. Preserve every supplied fact. Output only the finished webpage copy in 320-440 words total, including any headings.

Draft:
Foundry Yard Arts is accepting applications for its 2027 Material Stories Residency. The residency is for six artists who work with clay, fiber, wood, metal, paper, or combinations of those materials. Applicants may be emerging or established and must be at least 21 years old by January 10, 2027. They may live anywhere, but they must arrange their own travel to the fictional city of Bellwether. The residency runs March 8 through April 30, 2027. Each artist receives a private studio, shared access to basic fabrication tools, a furnished room in a nearby residence, a $2,400 stipend, and up to $600 in approved material reimbursement. Lunch is provided on weekdays. The facilities are step-free, and applicants can request additional access information before applying.

Residents are expected to work on site at least four days each week, join a ninety-minute group critique on Tuesdays, host one informal public studio visit, and give a ten-minute presentation during the closing gathering. Finished work is not required. The program does not take ownership of work made during the residency.

Applications require a 250-word project idea, a 150-word biography, five to eight work samples, a work-sample list, and one reference. There is no application fee. Applications close at 5 p.m. local time on January 10, 2027. A three-person panel of practicing artists reviews applications for clarity of inquiry, relationship to material practice, and likely contribution to the cohort. Decisions are emailed February 2. Questions go to residency@foundryyard.example. An online information session will be held December 12 at 6 p.m.; registration and the application form are at foundryyard.example/material-stories.
cases.outputConstraint: Output only 320-440 words total of finished webpage copy, including any headings; retain all program facts and avoid unsupported claims.

cases.title: Porchlight Reading Room Winter Appeal
cases.prompt:
Write a fundraising email from scratch for Porchlight Reading Room, a fictional nonprofit community study space.

Audience: previous small-dollar donors who have not donated in the past 12 months. They know the organization exists but may not know what its evening program does.

Campaign facts:
- Porchlight opens a supervised study room for students ages 12-18 on Tuesday, Wednesday, and Thursday evenings.
- The room is free to use and no school referral is required.
- During the most recent 10-week term, 86 students used the room at least once and the median nightly attendance was 24.
- The room provides desks, Wi-Fi, charging outlets, basic school supplies, hot tea, and fruit.
- Two paid coordinators and screened volunteers are present each evening.
- A winter gift of $32 covers one evening of supplies and snacks at the program's average attendance. This is a budgeting equivalence, not a restriction on how an individual gift is spent.
- The campaign goal is $9,600 by November 30 to fund the next 30 program evenings.
- Gifts of any amount are welcome.
- This organization, campaign, and all facts are fictional.

Write 320-450 words total, including both subject-line options, the preheader, body, P.S., and any labels. Provide two subject-line options, one preheader, and the email body. The email should quickly establish a concrete human reason to care, explain what happens in the room, make one clear donation request, and end with a brief P.S. that accurately explains the $32 equivalence. Use warm, direct language without manufacturing a named student, quotation, success story, emergency, matching gift, tax claim, or promise that donating will improve grades. Do not shame the reader or imply that a student will lose access if this recipient does not give. Keep logistical details subordinate to a readable emotional arc. Return only the subject lines, preheader, and finished email.
cases.outputConstraint: 320-450 words total including both subject options, preheader, body, P.S., and labels; clear donation request and emotional arc; no invented beneficiary, quotation, urgency device, outcome, matching gift, or tax claim; output only the finished email package.

cases.title: LumenBench Sample Tracker Webinar
cases.prompt:
Build a detailed outline for a 42-minute launch webinar introducing the fictional LumenBench sample tracker to managers of small environmental-testing laboratories. Start from the brief, not from existing prose. Output only a 620-820-word outline total, including all headings, labels, and table text. Include the audience tension, webinar promise, timed run of show, speaker roles, demonstration story, screen or visual cues, participation moments, transitions, proof boundaries, objection handling, call to action, and post-event follow-up assets. Supply sample phrasing only for the opening hook and final call to action; do not write a full script.

Brief:
LumenBench is a browser-based system for laboratories with three to twenty staff. It records a sample when it arrives, assigns a printed internal label, shows its current stage, stores handoff notes, and exports a chain-of-custody summary as PDF. The launch version has configurable stages, role types for admin, technician, and viewer, CSV import for client and project lists, and a read-only client status link that can be revoked. It does not connect to laboratory instruments, calculate test results, validate methods, generate regulatory submissions, or replace a laboratory information management system. It supports English only.

The fictional demonstration follows one water sample called Alder-17: it is logged at reception, assigned to metals testing, paused because the container seal is unclear, resumed after a supervisor note, moved to review, and marked complete. The demo should show the audit trail and the client status link without displaying personal data. A backup recorded demo is available if the live environment fails.

Speakers are product lead Mina Cho, laboratory operations adviser Rafael Ives, and moderator Tess Ward. Mina handles product context and screens; Rafael explains operational fit and boundaries; Tess manages time, poll, and questions. Evidence available: six fictional pilot laboratories used the product for eight weeks; five completed interviews said the shared stage view reduced the need to ask colleagues where a sample was; this is qualitative feedback, not a measured time saving. No customer logos, quotations, compliance certifications, or error-reduction statistics may be claimed.

Commercial facts: $59 per laboratory per month for up to ten users and 2,000 active samples; $119 per month for up to twenty users and 8,000 active samples; both plans include a 21-day trial without a payment card, email support, PDF export, and the client status link. There is no annual plan or enterprise tier. The call to action is to start a trial at lumenbench.example/trial. Attendees also receive a sample-intake checklist and a recording. Questions that cannot be answered confidently should be logged for written follow-up within two business days. Include an accessibility note: captions are live, slides use high contrast, questions can be submitted in writing, and the recording will include a corrected transcript.
cases.outputConstraint: Output only a 620-820-word webinar outline total, including all headings, labels, and table text; include only sample copy for the opening hook and closing call to action.

cases.title: Quiet Hours Feature Launch
cases.prompt:
Create a three-email product launch sequence from scratch for Lantern, a fictional mobile app that helps housemates coordinate shared chores.

Audience: existing free-plan users who have opened the app in the last 30 days.

Feature brief:
- The new feature is called Quiet Hours.
- A household can choose hours when non-urgent chore reminders are held and delivered afterward as one digest.
- A reminder explicitly marked urgent by a housemate is still delivered immediately.
- Each household has one shared Quiet Hours schedule; individual schedules are not supported.
- Any household member can change or disable the schedule, and the app records who changed it.
- Quiet Hours is included on free and paid plans.
- It becomes available on May 6 after users update to version 4.2.
- This app, feature, and all facts are fictional.

Write 400-500 words total, including all subject lines, preheaders, calls to action, and labels. For each email, include a subject line, preheader, and body. Email 1 should create recognition around the problem without naming the feature until the middle. Email 2 should launch the feature and explain how it works through a believable household scenario, but must not invent a testimonial or named customer. Email 3 should address the urgent-reminder and shared-schedule tradeoffs directly, then prompt the reader to update the app. Give the sequence a progression rather than repeating the same pitch. Use concise paragraphs and one call to action per email. Do not claim Quiet Hours blocks notifications, resolves conflict, uses AI, or guarantees peace. Avoid false scarcity, discounts, and claims about adoption or customer demand. Return only the complete email sequence.
cases.outputConstraint: 400-500 words total including all subject lines, preheaders, CTAs, and labels; exactly three emails, each with subject, preheader, body, and one CTA; distinct narrative progression; feature limits and May 6/version 4.2 requirement accurate; no invented proof, urgency, or unsupported outcomes; output only the sequence.

cases.title: Harbor Thread Cooperative Grant Review
cases.prompt:
Act as an exacting grant editor and review the fictional narrative below. The funder scores need, project logic, feasibility, measurement, community role, and clarity. Do not rewrite any passage and do not invent missing evidence. Output only a 650-820-word review total, including these headings: Overall assessment, Strongest elements, Gaps and credibility risks, Questions the draft must answer, and Revision sequence. Refer to concrete features of the draft, distinguish writing problems from evidence problems, and prioritize changes rather than listing generic advice.

Narrative draft:
Harbor Thread Cooperative requests $48,000 from the fictional Bellwether Making Fund to establish a shared textile-repair studio in the South Quay neighborhood. The cooperative began in 2023 when four costume makers started exchanging tools and overflow work. It now has twenty-seven dues-paying members, including alterations specialists, theatrical stitchers, upholsterers, and hobbyists. Members currently meet twice a month in a borrowed church hall where storage is prohibited and power is limited to four outlets. The proposed studio would operate in a vacant 1,100-square-foot storefront offered by the South Quay Community Land Trust under a three-year lease.

South Quay residents have told us repeatedly that they want an affordable place to repair clothes and learn practical skills. We know this from conversations at markets and from the popularity of repair videos online. The neighborhood has changed rapidly, and many families need ways to save money. A repair studio will reduce waste, create belonging, preserve knowledge, and transform the local economy. No other organization is doing exactly what we propose.

Grant funds would cover $18,000 for electrical work and accessibility improvements, $11,500 for eight refurbished industrial sewing machines and two worktables, $7,200 for the first year of rent, $4,800 for insurance and professional fees, $3,500 for teaching stipends, and $3,000 for signs, outreach, and supplies. The land trust has inspected the storefront but final contractor quotes are pending. The cooperative has $9,600 in unrestricted savings and expects member dues to contribute $6,000 during the first year. A local theater has offered four used machines, but their condition has not been assessed and they are not included in the equipment count.

The project will open in October 2027. From October through March, the studio will hold two public repair sessions each month, one six-week beginner sewing course, and one monthly member work night. Repair sessions will use a pay-what-you-can model. The beginner course will cost $90, with six free places reserved across two cohorts, although only one cohort is described in the schedule. Members can reserve studio time on other days. A part-time coordinator will manage bookings, supplies, and volunteers for ten hours a week. The budget does not yet show coordinator wages because the cooperative hopes a separate city application will cover them. If it does not, members will rotate the role.

We expect to serve 500 people in year one and divert 12,000 garments from landfill. Success will be measured by attendance, course completion, volunteer hours, participant stories, and photographs of repaired items. We will ask every visitor whether they learned something and whether they feel more connected to the neighborhood. The board will review results quarterly. We have not previously operated a public venue, but members have collectively taught more than sixty workshops for theaters, colleges, and private clients.

The cooperative is governed by a five-person board elected annually. Two board members live in South Quay, one works there, and two live elsewhere in the city. The cooperative plans to form a resident advisory circle after funding is confirmed. Advisory-circle members will meet quarterly and receive transit fare but no stipend. We will recruit them through the land trust, the primary school, and flyers. The project has letters of support from the land trust, the church that hosts current meetings, and the local theater.

The studio will be open Tuesday through Saturday, but exact public hours are still being discussed. The storefront entrance has one shallow step; proposed access work includes a permanent ramp and an accessible toilet, subject to landlord and permit approval. Classes will be held in English at launch. Members speak four additional languages and hope to add translated handouts later. The cooperative has general liability coverage for its current activity but needs a new policy for the venue.

By the end of the grant year, course fees, repair-session contributions, studio rentals, and increased dues will make the space self-sustaining. We project $42,000 in earned revenue but have not included the calculation here. If revenue is lower than expected, we will seek sponsors and hold a fundraising sale. The studio will prove that repair is not a niche activity but a movement capable of rebuilding South Quay from the inside out.
cases.outputConstraint: Output only a 650-820-word prioritized grant-editor review total, including the requested headings; do not rewrite or invent evidence.

cases.title: Oak & Iron Workshop Origin Film
cases.prompt:
Write a short founder-film script from scratch for Oak & Iron, a fictional repair workshop that restores household objects. The finished film will live on the workshop's About page and should run about four minutes.

Available story facts:
- Founder Mara Bell trained as a mechanical engineer and spent nine years designing small kitchen appliances.
- The idea began when a cracked hinge made her grandmother's 1970s stand mixer unusable, although its motor still worked. The manufacturer no longer supplied the hinge.
- Mara made a replacement hinge after four prototypes. The repaired mixer still works six years later.
- She opened Oak & Iron in a former print shop in the fictional city of Bellwether in 2022.
- The workshop repairs lamps, small appliances, clocks, hand tools, and selected furniture. It does not repair phones, computers, gas appliances, or items with severe mold.
- Every accepted object receives an assessment and a fixed quote before work begins. The assessment costs $24 and is credited toward an approved repair.
- In 2025, the four-person workshop assessed 1,140 objects and completed 782 repairs. An item being assessed does not mean it can or should be repaired.
- Customers may decline a quote and collect the item.
- Oak & Iron offers a free monthly clinic where visitors can learn basic maintenance; clinic places are limited and must be reserved.
- The business, founder, place, and all facts are fictional.

Write 600-750 words total, including scene numbers, direction labels, and any on-screen text. Format the script in 8-10 numbered scenes. Each scene must contain a VISUAL direction, an AUDIO direction, and either MARA narration or concise on-screen text. Begin on an intriguing physical detail rather than a company introduction. Reveal the broken mixer as the origin of the business, then widen the story to the present workshop and its repair philosophy. Include the important service boundaries and pricing without letting the middle feel like a catalog. End on a concrete image and an understated invitation to request an assessment or reserve a clinic place. Do not invent dialogue from customers or Mara's grandmother, additional biography, environmental impact figures, testimonials, awards, guarantees, or dramatic business hardship. Do not imply every object should be saved. Keep production feasible inside one workshop with Mara, staff hands, and objects; do not require archival footage or outside locations. Return only the finished script.
cases.outputConstraint: 600-750 words total including scene numbers, direction labels, and on-screen text; 8-10 numbered scenes with VISUAL, AUDIO, and narration or on-screen text in each; factual origin-to-present arc; feasible production; honest service boundaries and assessment terms; no invented biography, dialogue, proof, impact, or guarantee; output only the script.

cases.title: A Respectful Weekend on Bracken Island
cases.prompt:
Rewrite the fictional visitor-guide draft below into a vivid, practical guide titled "A Respectful Weekend on Bracken Island." Give the reader a sense of place while keeping responsible behavior concrete rather than preachy. Preserve all stated logistics, restrictions, names, and times. Do not add attractions, history, wildlife facts, or environmental claims. Output only the finished guide in 650-820 words total, including the title, headings, and ending-checklist labels.

Draft:
Bracken Island is a fictional, car-free island reached by the twenty-five-minute Mossline ferry from Port Alder. Friday ferries leave at 4:40 p.m. and 7:10 p.m.; Saturday ferries leave at 8:15 a.m., 11:30 a.m., and 3:20 p.m. Return sailings on Sunday are 12:10 p.m., 4:30 p.m., and 7 p.m. Tickets should be reserved online. The ferry carries bicycles for an added fee, but e-bikes must have removable batteries. Visitors should bring a reusable water bottle because shops do not sell single-use plastic water bottles.

The village is a seven-minute walk from the dock. It has two inns, a twelve-bed hostel, three cafes, a grocery cooperative, a bakery, and a small gear-repair shop. Lodging should be booked before ferry tickets. The cooperative closes at 6 p.m. Friday, 4 p.m. Saturday, and noon Sunday. The bakery opens at 7 a.m. and usually sells out of savory rolls before 10. Visitors staying at the hostel need to bring a towel.

The island loop trail is eleven kilometers and takes most walkers four to five hours. The path crosses working sheep fields. Walkers must close every gate, keep dogs leashed, and step aside for farm vehicles. There are no trash bins after the village, so all waste must be carried back. The western cliff section closes in winds above 55 kilometers per hour; a red flag at the trail kiosk means use the signed inland bypass. The tidal spur to Lantern Shelf is accessible only during the safe window posted daily at the kiosk. Visitors must not attempt it outside that window.

Saturday alternatives include a ninety-minute basket workshop at Reed House at 10 a.m. and 2 p.m.; it has eight places and requires advance booking. The community archive opens from 1 to 4 p.m. and asks visitors to leave wet coats at the entrance. The sheltered cove beside North Steps is open to swimmers, but there is no lifeguard. The working pier and the freshwater reservoir are not visitor areas. Drones are prohibited everywhere on the island unless the council has issued written permission.

Quiet hours in the village are 10 p.m. to 7 a.m. The Signal Room cafe hosts an acoustic session Saturday from 7:30 to 9:30 p.m.; entry is free, seating is first come, and visitors should not record performers without asking. On Sunday morning, volunteers lead a beach clean from 9 to 10:30, meeting outside the ferry office; gloves and bags are provided. Participation is optional.

Weather changes quickly. Visitors should pack a waterproof layer and shoes suitable for muddy paths. Mobile service is unreliable beyond the village. A paper map is available at the ferry office. The island medical post handles minor incidents from 9 a.m. to 5 p.m.; emergencies should be reported to the local emergency number shown on ferry tickets and trail signs. Accessibility notes: the ferry and village route are step-free; the loop trail is uneven and includes steep sections; Reed House has a ramp and accessible toilet. More detailed access information is available at brackenisland.example/access. The guide should end with a compact checklist that includes bookings, refillable bottle, weather layer, footwear, offline or paper map, and carrying waste out.
cases.outputConstraint: Output only the finished guide in 650-820 words total, including the title, headings, and ending-checklist labels; use exclusively the supplied facts.

cases.title: Harborline Difficult Conversations Workshop
cases.prompt:
Create a sales page from scratch for a fictional live online workshop called Clear Before Clever: Writing Difficult Workplace Messages.

Audience: first-time managers and experienced individual contributors who need to write messages about missed deadlines, changed priorities, declined requests, and mistakes. They are thoughtful professionals, not aggressive closers, and they dislike manipulative business copy.

Offer facts:
- The workshop is led by fictional facilitator Ren Ito, who has spent 11 years editing internal communications for hospitals, universities, and software companies.
- It is a live, 2.5-hour online session for 12-24 participants.
- Participants practice on fictional scenarios, never confidential workplace material.
- The session covers identifying the real decision, separating facts from interpretation, stating impact without accusation, making a specific request, and editing for tone after clarity.
- Participants complete two short exercises, revise one full message, and receive a printable decision-and-draft worksheet.
- The workshop does not provide legal, HR, crisis-communications, or mental-health advice.
- The next session is October 17 from 1:00-3:30 p.m. Eastern.
- The price is $165 per person. Registration closes October 15 or when 24 places are filled.
- A participant may cancel for a full refund until October 10. After that, the place may be transferred to another person for this session.
- The workshop, facilitator, credentials, date, and all facts are fictional.

Write 700-850 words total, including headings, FAQ questions, and call-to-action labels. Include: a headline and subhead; an opening that dramatizes the moment before sending a difficult message without inventing a customer story; a section naming who the workshop is and is not for; a concrete account of what participants will do during the session; a five-part curriculum section; a brief facilitator section; a candid boundaries section; date, format, capacity, price, cancellation, and transfer details; a compact FAQ with exactly four questions; and a final call to action. Use persuasive narrative and specificity, not pressure. Anticipate the objection that good intentions alone should be enough and the objection that a template can solve the problem. Do not invent testimonials, employers, certifications, completion statistics, bonuses, guarantees, discounts, or scarcity beyond the actual 24-person capacity and registration deadline. Do not promise promotions, conflict resolution, legal safety, or a particular workplace outcome. Return only the finished sales page.
cases.outputConstraint: 700-850 words total including headings, FAQ questions, and CTA labels; all specified sales-page sections, exactly four FAQ questions, complete logistics, and honest offer boundaries; persuasive without unsupported outcomes, invented proof, false scarcity, or pressure; output only the finished page.

cases.title: The Index of Missing Things
cases.prompt:
Create a rigorous season outline for an original six-episode mystery audio drama titled "The Index of Missing Things." Start from the premise and story constraints below; there is no draft to rewrite. Output only the outline in 1,000-1,300 words total, including all headings, labels, and table text. Include: a one-paragraph series engine; tone and listener promise; the central dramatic question; concise character arcs; rules governing the mystery; a six-episode table or clearly labeled sequence with opening image, major turns, revelation, emotional movement, and cliffhanger for each episode; clue distribution and fair-play logic; sound motifs; and a final section showing what resolves and what remains productively open. Do not write finished scenes, dialogue, or promotional copy. Do not introduce supernatural explanations, crimes more serious than those supplied, or additional major characters.

Premise:
In the fictional coastal town of Orison, municipal archivist Mara Venn discovers that several ordinary objects described in a handwritten 1986 index are absent from both the archive and public memory: a brass room key, a child's rain boot, a restaurant menu, a spool of green thread, and a cassette labeled "Low Tide." Each object points to one week when a proposed seawall divided the town. The seawall was never built. The official record says the council withdrew it after a budget review, but meeting minutes for that week have been replaced by blank pages carrying valid archive stamps.

Characters:
Mara, 38, values documented proof and avoids discussing why she left Orison at eighteen. Her late mother, Elia, had catalogued volunteer donations to the archive in 1986. Jonah Pike, 41, repairs marine radios and remembers Mara leaving without saying goodbye; he distrusts institutions but compulsively records local sounds. Council clerk Dev Rusk, 62, began as a junior clerk in 1986 and insists the blanks are a preservation error. Anika Sol, 29, runs a community kitchen in the former Tideglass restaurant and wants the past investigated only if it helps people living in Orison now. Ruth Bell, 74, donated many archive materials and speaks carefully because she once promised Elia not to identify a confidential source. No other major characters may be added.

Underlying truth, fixed for the outline:
The proposed seawall would have protected the tourist quarter while redirecting storm water toward forty-two lower-income homes. Elia learned that the engineering appendix describing the redirected risk had been removed from the public packet. With Ruth's help, she staged a temporary "misfiling" of five mundane donated objects. The catalog descriptions formed a retrieval trail to duplicate technical pages hidden in the Tideglass restaurant's sealed dumbwaiter. Before Elia could reveal them, the council quietly withdrew the seawall for political reasons unrelated to ethics. Dev, then a junior clerk, was ordered to replace the relevant minutes with blank stamped sheets. He complied and has concealed that act, but he did not remove the engineering appendix and does not know where the duplicates are. No one was killed or physically harmed. The present mystery concerns concealment, civic memory, loyalty, and whether evidence can still serve the town.

Object and clue rules:
The brass key once opened the Tideglass staff entrance and now sits inside Jonah's box of inherited radio parts. The single rain boot was used as a playful container for a rolled map and was later discarded; a photograph in Ruth's donation shows its distinctive repaired heel beside the archive steps. The menu contains tiny pencil numbers that correspond to tide-table columns, not a cipher invented from nowhere. The green thread matches binding repairs in the 1986 engineering appendix and remains caught behind the archive's map cabinet. The "Low Tide" cassette is one of Jonah's late father's ambient recordings; beneath harbor noise it captures Elia and Ruth testing the dumbwaiter, but their words are incomplete and must not solve the mystery alone. The duplicate pages remain in the dumbwaiter. Every decisive conclusion must follow from at least two clues.

Season progression constraints:
Episode 1 begins with Mara reading the strange index during a storm warning and ends when the valid stamps are found on blank minutes. Episode 2 centers on the key and Mara's strained reunion with Jonah. Episode 3 uses the rain-boot photograph and introduces Anika's concern that the archive is turning neighborhood pain into a puzzle. Episode 4 centers on the menu, tide columns, and the incomplete cassette. Episode 5 forces Dev's admission about replacing the minutes but establishes that his confession does not identify the missing appendix or duplicates. Episode 6 searches the former Tideglass building, finds the dumbwaiter pages, and ends with a public listening-and-reading event rather than a punitive confrontation. Mara admits she left because Elia's devotion to public work made home feel secondary; she chooses to remain for a limited six-month project, not forever. Jonah shares his recordings under community-controlled terms. Anika secures agreement to use the recovered history in current flood-planning workshops. Ruth may identify herself as Elia's helper only after the documents are safe. Dev must face public accountability, but the ending should avoid easy absolution or villainy.

Audio and structure constraints:
The drama is documentary-adjacent but entirely fictional. Sound can convey archive carts, rain gutters, radio static, cassette hiss, kitchen preparation, tide bells, and the dumbwaiter's counterweight. Narration should be limited; discovery should occur largely through action and conversation. Each episode should run about 28 minutes and contain one earned reversal. Listeners must receive the clues before or alongside Mara, never only in retrospective explanation. The emotional climax is the community deciding how to use the recovered evidence, not merely the physical discovery.
cases.outputConstraint: Output only a 1,000-1,300-word season outline total, including all headings, labels, and table text, with all requested sections; no finished scenes, dialogue, promotional copy, or new major characters.

cases.title: The Last Weather Station
cases.prompt:
Write an original literary speculative-fiction story from scratch using the setup and constraints below. Everything is fictional.

Premise:
- Forty years from now, most local weather forecasting is generated from orbital sensors and privately owned prediction systems.
- Seventy-two-year-old Inez Vale is the sole caretaker of Station 14, an old mountaintop weather station scheduled to be decommissioned at dawn.
- Her adult son Tomas arrives during the final night to drive her down before a snowstorm. They have spoken only through brief monthly calls for the past three years.
- Inez's late partner, Dev, also worked at the station. Dev died eight years earlier.
- The official reason for closing Station 14 is cost: its instruments duplicate newer systems.
- That evening, the station's mechanical barometer begins dropping faster than the network forecast predicts. The network still shows the storm passing east of the only mountain road.
- Inez does not believe the old instrument is magically infallible. She knows its calibration can drift, but she also knows wind striking this particular ridge creates a pressure pattern the network has missed twice before.
- If she reports a possible error, an automated dispatch system may close the road and delay a medical-supply shipment serving three valley clinics. If she stays silent and the storm crosses the road, the driver could be trapped.

Required story movement:
- Open with Inez performing one precise closing task while Tomas waits impatiently, making the emotional and physical setting immediate.
- Establish their estrangement through action, subtext, and selective dialogue rather than an explanatory summary.
- Let Tomas initially see the disputed reading as one more reason his mother cannot leave the station behind.
- Reveal that their deeper conflict is not that Inez missed Dev's funeral; she attended. It is that, during Dev's final illness, Inez kept submitting observations from the station because Dev had asked her to preserve an uninterrupted fifty-year record. Tomas believes she used duty to avoid being present even when she was physically in the room.
- Force Inez to choose whether to report the reading before certainty is possible. Her choice must have a real cost and cannot vindicate her completely.
- The storm's path should ultimately justify caution, but one clinic must have to postpone non-emergency appointments because the shipment is delayed. Do not make anyone die or suffer a miraculous rescue.
- End after Inez and Tomas leave the station, with a changed but incomplete understanding between them. The final image must involve the blank line that will follow Station 14's last entry in the regional weather record.

Craft constraints:
- Write 1100-1400 words total, including the title, with no section headings.
- Use close third person centered on Inez and past tense.
- Balance external tension, sensory mountain detail, and restrained family conflict.
- Give Inez and Tomas distinct dialogue without turning either person into a villain or spokesperson.
- Make the technical elements understandable in context; do not add pseudo-scientific jargon or impossible instrument behavior.
- Dev may appear only through memory and objects, not as a ghost, recording, letter, or newly discovered message.
- Avoid a surprise conspiracy, a sentimental reconciliation, a speech explaining the theme, or an ending in which the old ways simply defeat the new.
- Do not reuse recognizable characters, settings, or sentences from existing fiction.
- Return only the finished story.
cases.outputConstraint: 1100-1400 words total including the title; titled story with no section headings; close-third Inez perspective in past tense; all required plot movements and ambiguous cost; technically plausible weather elements; restrained, incomplete reconciliation; no conspiracy, ghost, discovered message, miracle, or simplistic old-versus-new ending; output only the story.

cases.title: SignalNest Incident Workspace
cases.prompt:
Rewrite the fictional landing-page draft below into a complete, credible page for SignalNest, an incident workspace for small software teams. Make the value clear through specificity, not hype. Vary the pacing, give each section a job, and address practical objections. Preserve all facts and limitations. Do not invent customer quotes, certifications, integrations, performance statistics, security guarantees, or savings claims. Output only the finished landing-page copy in 1,000-1,250 words total, including all headings, call-to-action labels, and FAQ questions. Include a headline and subhead, a primary call to action, problem framing, a concise how-it-works section, feature sections grounded in the facts, an honest fit/not-fit section, pricing, security and data handling, onboarding, a short FAQ, and a closing call to action.

Draft and source facts:
SignalNest is a fictional browser-based incident workspace for software teams of five to forty people. It puts the incident timeline, owners, decisions, customer-facing updates, and follow-up tasks in one shared room. It was designed for teams that currently coordinate incidents across chat messages, video calls, documents, and ticket trackers. SignalNest does not monitor infrastructure, detect incidents, page responders, replace an observability platform, or automatically fix problems.

A user opens an incident room manually or through one of two included connections: an incoming webhook or the SignalNest command for the fictional chat tool Lattice. Every room begins with five fields: current status, incident lead, customer impact, next update time, and active workstreams. Team members can add time-stamped timeline entries, assign an owner to a workstream, record a decision with a short rationale, and mark information as internal-only or approved for a public update. The room keeps an edit history showing what changed, when, and by whom.

The update composer turns facts already marked approved into a draft status-page message. It never publishes automatically. A human must review, edit if needed, and click copy. The composer does not infer cause or impact and does not use internal-only entries. Teams can save their own opening, update, monitoring, and resolved templates. SignalNest ships with neutral example templates, but teams are expected to adapt language to their service and policies.

During an incident, the focus view hides completed workstreams and older timeline detail while keeping status, lead, current customer impact, next update time, and unresolved work visible. A separate observer link gives authorized colleagues a read-only view without letting them edit or join the response channel. Observer access can be revoked at any time. Each incident room can be exported as JSON or as a formatted Markdown record. Follow-up tasks can be exported as CSV. There is no direct ticket-tracker integration in the current version.

After resolution, SignalNest creates a review workspace from the existing timeline, decisions, and workstreams. It provides section prompts for impact, detection, response, contributing conditions, what helped, and follow-up work. It does not write conclusions or assign blame. Editors can rearrange entries, remove irrelevant detail, add context, and export the completed review to Markdown.

SignalNest has two plans. Team costs $39 per workspace per month and includes up to fifteen members, unlimited incident rooms, one observer link per active incident, thirty-day room history, JSON and Markdown exports, and email support. Studio costs $89 per workspace per month and includes up to forty members, up to five observer links per active incident, one-year room history, custom templates, CSV task export, priority email support, and basic role controls for admin, responder, and observer. Both plans have a fourteen-day free trial and require no payment card to begin. Annual billing is not offered. Additional seats are not available; teams that exceed a plan limit must move to the next plan.

Data is hosted in the fictional Northlake region. Data is encrypted in transit and at rest. SignalNest staff access is restricted to designated support and operations personnel and is logged. Customers can delete an incident room or the entire workspace from the admin screen. Workspace deletion enters a seven-day recovery window and is then permanently removed from active systems; encrypted backups expire within thirty days. SignalNest currently supports email-and-password login with optional time-based one-time passwords. It does not currently support SSO, SCIM, customer-managed encryption keys, private cloud deployment, data residency choices, or regulated-health-data workflows. It has completed an independent penetration test, and a summary is available to trial users on request. No compliance certification should be claimed.

The typical setup is: create a workspace, invite the response team, choose the default time zone, adapt at least one update template, connect the incoming webhook or Lattice command if desired, and run a fifteen-minute practice incident. A sample incident called Broken Sundial is included and can be deleted. Documentation includes a setup checklist, a facilitation guide for the practice incident, and examples of useful timeline entries. SignalNest offers no live onboarding calls, migration service, or custom implementation.

The intended buyers are engineering managers, reliability leads, and technical operations leads at small software companies. It may also suit agencies that manage several small services within one internal team. It is not intended for large enterprises needing complex permissions, organizations that require automatic paging or infrastructure monitoring, or teams that need regulated-data support. The main call to action is Start a 14-day trial. The secondary call to action is View the sample incident. Use the fictional URLs signalnest.example/start and signalnest.example/sample.

FAQ facts: users may cancel at any time and retain access through the current monthly billing period; exports remain available while the workspace is active; trial workspaces are deleted thirty days after the trial ends unless converted to paid; a workspace uses one default time zone but each timeline entry also stores its UTC time; support hours are Monday-Friday, 9 a.m.-5 p.m. Northlake time, excluding regional holidays; the product interface and support are available only in English.
cases.outputConstraint: Output only 1,000-1,250 words total of finished landing-page copy, including all headings, CTA labels, and FAQ questions; preserve limitations and make no unsupported claims.
